title: The Macro Minute: How tariffs impact the US economy, part II? What’s the latest on global liquidity?

Happy Thursday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here at Darius Dell to
present our macro minute for Thursday,
August 14th, 2025. Hope everyone's
having a great week. So, as always,
we'll start with the executive summary
from today's leadoff morning note. If
you would like to review the analysis
according to these conclusions and to
see if you need to make any changes in
your portfolio to remain on the right
side of market risk or to get on the
right side of market risk to the extent
you haven't been following Kiss or Dr.
Mo, you need to be a client of 42 macro.
So today's macro questions are how are
tariffs impacting the US economy part
two and what's the latest regarding
global liquidity? Our answers the July
PPI, July median CPI and July trim mean
CPI data each supported our sticky
inflation theme. Most concerning the
July PPI data featured significant
positive deltas that imply there is a
pig in the python that will cause future
inflation data to accelerate over the
medium term. Moreover, this process will
not be linear as it will be and and it
will not be quick because companies have
definitely front-loaded inventory orders
amid tariff rate volatility. Recall that
effective tariff rates have gone both up
and down year to date. All told,
investors should expect the following
three things to occur over the next 12
to 18 months. Number one, reported
inflation is heading higher, but
generally not at a speed faster than
consensus already anticipates. Today's
PBI beat notwithstanding. Number two,
the timing of tariff related price
increases will be nonlinear and vary
across sectors and companies based on
profit margin cushions. And number
three, at some point the tariff impulse
will wear off and inflation will start
heading lower again. The FOMC will be
foolish to overreact to these
proactively predictable dynamics with an
illtime defense of their arbitrary 2%
inflation target.
After the best fourmon stretch of growth
since late 2022, early 2023 with three
month SARS in the um 11 to 15% range,
global liquidity is now expanding only
4% on a three-month annualized basis.
While that's good enough to buttress
risk assets through a seasonally weak
period for asset markets, the firming of
the US dollar today is a reminder that
the PAL Fed's fifth major policy mistake
represents a short to medium-term
headwind for risk assets in global
liquidity terms. This is because the
uptrend in global liquidity in recent
quarters has been primarily driven by
the revaluation of global money supply
and global FX reserves which themselves
have been flattered by the decline in
the US dollar. While the US dollar may
firm in the coming weeks or months until
pow capitulates, investors should expect
the dollar to continue breaking down
over the long term if we continue to be
right on our call for regime change at
the Fed. to this point, Treasury
Secretary Scott Besson's commentary on
Bloomberg television yesterday uh was
quite telling and um just a couple
highlights from that. So he thinks the
Fed should go 50 basis points in
September. Uh it's essentially saying,
"Hey, if we got the if the labor market
data was accurate the last few months,
we would have cut already basically." Uh
and then uh he did say he thinks the
policy rate should be about 150 to 175
basis points uh lower uh than it
currently is. So uh you know, obviously
we're not going to get there in a
straight line. It's going to be some
regime change in the fact that needs to
continue to take place. Not in both in
terms of changing the the the bodies on
the FOMC and the Fed board of governors,
but also changing the minds of the
people that are already there. Right?
These are human beings that can be
swayed by, you know, thoughtful,
credible analysis. Hopefully, hopefully
that can be swayed by thoughtful
credible analysis, the kind of which
we've been producing in recent months
that essentially says the Fed is making
a policy mistake primarily through the
lens of their arbitrary 2% inflation
target that they got from some dead
central bank in New Zealand. So, uh, you
know, I think we're going to be continue
to be right on this and over the long
term, it's probably likely to result in
a substantially weaker US dollar,
substantially higher levels of global
liquidity. But again, the path to
realizing Paradigm C's upside risk is
not linear. So just be aware of that. Uh
so uh transition jar uh 42 micro
dashboard. As always wrap up the
question from our community. Uh this
one's just a comment here. I'll just
read it. Um says, "As someone who with
his own who has his own struggles with
loss aversion, I try to remember Peter
Lynch's famous quote. Far more money has
been lost by investors preparing for
corrections than has been lost in the
corrections themselves." Ain't that
true? It's comfortable to sit on the
sidelines and watch markets correct, but
then they usually go much higher.
Markets rise about 85% of the time. KISS
earned my confidence because of the
level um to to my level of
understanding, KISS operates as a loss
limit, ensuring that you don't lose much
even if you're buying at the top. That
is correct. And at the same time, uh not
participating in stock markets is risky
because the cost of everything keeps
going up even if your wealth does not.
Let me say that again. Not participating
uh in stock markets is a risk because
the cost of everything keeps going up
even if your wealth does not. The
financial media has most people focus on
downside risks, not realizing that
sitting on the sidelines is sometimes a
bigger risk. So if you aren't totally
sold yet, take a portion of your
portfolio and commit to KISS for six
months and then you can reevaluate then.
Hope that is helpful. So um again I
don't this is their words not mine. You
know what I mean?
telling you guys for years now that KISS
is the best most effective retirement
solution out there because it it helps
you maximize upside capture in bull
markets and minimize downside capture in
bare markets. You know the problem with
the traditional you know some you know
kind of the pocket of the old money Wall
Street folks is they they help you
maximize upside capture bull markets but
then they cause you to you know maximize
downside capture bare markets too. So
your portfolio and and your stress and
your stress levels and the volatility
that you're experiencing at home and at
work, you know, are along for that very
volatile ride, you know, and then you
got the macro cohort of people who
became popular after the global
financial crisis that essentially get
paid to scare you into subscribing to
their new bare porn newsletters to keep
you out of the market. So they're
minimizing upside capture and bull
markets while also minimizing downside
capture and bare markets. And so both of
those are wrong. Both of those are
wrong. What you want to do instead is
what KISS does for thousands of clients
around the world, which is maximize
upside capture in bull markets and
minimize downside capture in bare
markets. That's it. You don't have to
make investing any more complicated than
that. If that's what you need, if you
you do that those two things for a long
enough period of time, which is exactly
what KISS was expressly designed to do
by someone with 17 years of
institutional macro risk management
experience servicing many of the top
portfolio managers and CIOS around the
world with the exact same research, the
exact same risk management signals, the
exact same models. We built KISS for you
and for myself selfishly. I used KISS to
manage my entire liquid net worth. And
you want to know why? Because I looked
around and every retirement solution I
started checking out said it was was not
good. It was not effective enough.
Certainly not relative to the kinds of,
you know, uh, return distributions that
I help institutional investors generate
and deliver to their clients. And so I'm
like, well, I guess I got to build that
for retail for myself and for retail
audience. And guess what? It's been
working fantastically. This will be the
third consecutive year KISS has
demolishedly outperformed investor
consensus and I don't expect it will be
the last. So I hope everyone is having a
wonderful day. We'll catch you back here
tomorrow. Cheers.
